1:
The chapter opens with a heading — "Now these are the divisions of the sons of Aaron" — and the witnesses read it first as a matter of order and then as a matter of grace. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the Hebrew flatly, "And for the sons of Aaron, their divisions were as follows," calling it the superscription to the whole section that runs down to v.19; as usual, he remarks, the writer starts ab ovo, from the very egg, having already named Aaron's sons at 1 Chron. 6:3 and Exod. 6:23. Keil and Delitzsch supply the reason the roll must begin with a genealogy: of Aaron's four sons the two elder, Nadab and Abihu, died before their father leaving no children, so that only Eleazar and Ithamar entered upon the priesthood, and it is their two houses that David is now to divide "according to their service."

The reformed John Gill takes the word "divisions" simply as the classes or courses that follow, and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley as the several branches into which Aaron's family was parted; Adam Clarke, of the same school, sketches the plan of the chapter in advance — David divides the families of Eleazar and Ithamar by lot into twenty-four courses through v.19, and the rest of the sons of Levi are ordered from v.20 to the end.

It is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who lifts the bare heading into doctrine. The particular account, he grants, is of little use to us now; yet when Ezra published it after the captivity it served to guide the church's affairs back into their old channel. The word "divisions," he is careful to add, does not mean that the sons of Aaron were at variance — a pity there should ever be such divisions among the sons of Israel, and worse among the sons of Aaron — but that their work was distributed, and distributed by God himself, for the regular discharge of the office; for God is the God of order and not of confusion, above all in his worship, as every member of the mystical body has its use for the good of the whole (Rom. 12:4-5; 1 Cor. 12:12). Above all Henry looks ahead to the eighth course, that of Abijah at v.10, which the Gospel names as the course of Zacharias, father of John the Baptist (Luke 1:5) — proof that these courses, though broken in the bad reigns and by the exile, continued in succession down to the destruction of the second temple. Each course bore the name of its founder, and the high priest himself is called Aaron at v.19, as Christ is now high priest over the house of God, to whom all believers, made priests, are in subjection.
2:
The genealogy pauses at once over a grave, and the witnesses agree that the death of Aaron's two elder sons is the hinge on which the whole arrangement turns. The reformed John Gill notes only that Nadab and Abihu "died before their father," while Aaron was yet living; the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds the sequel — Eleazar and Ithamar served the priest's office during their father's lifetime, and after his death Eleazar succeeded to the high priesthood, though under Eli, long after, the family of Ithamar re-entered that dignity. It is a history the chapter presupposes without telling: the office had wandered between the two surviving lines, and David's division must now do justice to both.

The grammatical-historical witnesses fill in why the elder pair fell. Charles Ellicott points the reader to Lev. 10:1-2, which tells the reason plainly — they offered "strange fire" before the LORD and were consumed — and observes that the notice here appears to be drawn from Num. 3:4, whose fuller form adds that Eleazar and Ithamar ministered "before the face of Aaron their father." The clause "had no children," he renders literally, "and sons had not been born to them," so that the elder branch simply died out and the priesthood was left, of necessity, with the two houses of Eleazar and Ithamar. Keil and Delitzsch read it the same way, citing Lev. 10:1 and Num. 3:4, and treating the verse as the ground for the twofold division David is about to make.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the practical point that the two survivors "were the only persons to whom the execution of that office was committed." The witnesses do not divide. Together they let a single sombre fact carry its weight: the whole priesthood of Israel, and the whole apparatus of the twenty-four courses that follows, descended from just two of Aaron's four sons, because presumption at the altar had cut off the other two without an heir. The order of the temple was built, quite literally, over the memory of a judgment, and the reader is reminded at the outset that nearness to holy things is no light thing.
3:
With the third verse the work of ordering begins, and the witnesses attend both to who did the dividing and to a genuine puzzle in one of the names. The reformed John Gill states the plain sense: David distributed the posterities of Eleazar and Ithamar into their several classes or courses. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the grammar, observing that "Zadok of the sons of Eleazar, and Ahimelech of the sons of Ithamar" is part of the subject, so that the verse means "David, with Zadok and Ahimelech, divided them" — the two heads of the priestly houses assisting the king in the classification "according to their official class," the same phrase, he notes, as at 1 Chron. 13:3 and 1 Chron. 23:11. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the division as allotting to each course its several times, "wherein they should by turns have the government of holy ministrations."

The knot lies in the name Ahimelech. Clarke, of the wesleyan-arminian school, holds that "Ahimelech is put here for Abiathar, who was high priest in the days of David"; Abiathar, he explains on Calmet's authority, bore the name Ahimelech as well, as his father had before him, so that the man named here is the famous high priest under another title. Keil and Delitzsch, with the plain text, take him rather as "Ahimelech the son of Abiathar," a distinct man — the son standing in for the father in the priestly business — and Ellicott follows the same reading. So a small debate stands: is the colleague of Zadok the high priest Abiathar himself, doubly named, or his son Ahimelech acting in his room?

Beneath the philology the witnesses share one point. The division was not David's solitary act of royal will but a joint work of throne and priesthood, the king dividing the courses in concert with the recognised heads of both surviving lines. Wesley's phrase captures the aim — that each course might "by turns" hold the government of the holy ministrations — and Ellicott's careful parsing keeps the honour where the text places it, upon Zadok and Ahimelech together, the representatives of Eleazar and of Ithamar, that neither house might feel the arrangement imposed upon it from without.
4:
The fourth verse explains why the two houses were not divided evenly, and the witnesses fix on a single principle of reckoning. The reformed John Gill notes that there were "more chief men," that is, more heads of fathers' houses and families, found among the sons of Eleazar than among the sons of Ithamar. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott makes that principle the key to the whole arrangement: the basis of division was not the individual members of the several families but the heads of them. "There were more head men, or heads of households, deriving from Eleazar than from Ithamar," he writes; and since the heads were more numerous, the total number of priests claiming descent from Eleazar must likewise have been the greater.

Ellicott is careful with the two Hebrew idioms the verse employs. "Chief men" is literally "heads of the men," meaning the heads of single families or households; whereas "heads of the fathers" denotes the heads of whole groups of fathers, or clans — a distinction he illustrates from the mustering language of Josh. 7:14 and the verses that follow it, where Israel is sifted by tribes, families, and households in turn. The upshot is the tally the verse records: to the sons of Eleazar, sixteen heads of fathers' houses; to the sons of Ithamar, eight. And they — David together with the two high priests of v.3 — divided them accordingly.

The witnesses draw no controversy from so plain a verse, but they let its quiet fairness show. The uneven division of sixteen to eight was not favouritism but arithmetic: the courses were apportioned to the houses in proportion to the heads each could muster, so that the larger line received the larger number of courses and the smaller line its due eight. The whole scheme of twenty-four, the reader begins to see, rests on a real census of living households, not on an artificial symmetry; the number was reached by counting the fathers, and the God who would order the service by lot ordered it first by an honest reckoning of the men who were there to serve.
5:
Here the method is named — "thus were they divided by lot" — and the witnesses gather to explain both the mechanics and the meaning of the casting of lots. The reformed John Gill sees in it a simple wisdom: the lot decided which course should serve first and which next, and who should be chief to each, so that the whole was settled "to prevent envy, strife, and contention about precedence," each course serving its week and then going out. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agrees that the lot "prevented jealousies," for the families were all equally noble and had equal right to the distinctions; and John Wesley, of the same school, grounds it higher — the lot was used "that the disposal thereof might be of the Lord," so that no man could be charged with partiality nor complain of wrong. Wesley recalls how Matthias too was chosen to the apostleship by lot with prayer, and adds, with Henry, that he knew not but it might still be used in faith, as an instituted ordinance.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses that the two lines drew "these with those" — the clans of Eleazar and of Ithamar standing apart, the lots drawn for each alternately — the object being to decide "the question of precedence in the order of ministration," the liturgical functions being of course the same for all, as the Gospel's picture of the courses at Luke 1:8-9 assumes. Keil and Delitzsch explain why the matter was left to the lot at all: since "holy princes and princes of God" had arisen from both houses — the high-priesthood belonging chiefly to Eleazar's line, yet held for a season in the judges' days by Ithamar's — neither branch was to have an advantage, and the order was fixed for both by the impartial lot.

Over the striking phrase "governors of the house of God" the witnesses reflect a little. Ellicott hears in "princes of the sanctuary" the equivalent of the "princes of the priests" of 2 Chron. 36:14, and in "princes of God" — a phrase found nowhere else — either the same men or, more likely, the high priests, comparing the title given to Abraham as a "mighty prince" at Gen. 23:6 and the "princes of the sanctuary" of Isa. 43:28. Wesley reads it more broadly still, of "the things of God" — all the persons and ministrations of the sanctuary, as distinguished from "the king's matters" of 2 Chron. 19:11. The witnesses do not contend; they converge on one confession: that the fairest ordering of holy service was reached by handing the decision, out of all reach of favour, to the God who disposes the lot.
6:
A notary is named, and the witnesses linger over the solemnity of a decision set down in writing before witnesses. The reformed John Gill identifies the recorder as "Shemaiah the son of Nethaneel the scribe," passing on the Targum's fancy that this was Moses the chief scribe under another name. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, give the fuller scene: Shemaiah, one of the Levites, wrote down the classes as the lot determined them, and wrote them "before the king, and the princes, and Zadok the priest, and Ahimelech the son of Abiathar, and before the chief of the fathers of the priests and Levites" — the whole assembly standing as guarantee that nothing was tampered with.

On the manner of the drawing the witnesses take slightly different roads through a difficult Hebrew clause. Keil and Delitzsch read "one father's-house drawn for Eleazar, and drawn for Ithamar," wrestling honestly with a text they confess obscure. Charles Ellicott judges the clause corrupt and would restore it by the change of a single letter to read "one clan drawn for Eleazar, and one drawn for Ithamar" — that is, alternately — noting that the Septuagint has "one by one for Eleazar, and one by one for Ithamar." He observes the arithmetic that made such alternation necessary: since Eleazar's heads were twice as many, the chances were that the Ithamarites would all be drawn before Eleazar's line was exhausted, exactly as, in the parallel roll of the singers, ten "sons of the Hemanite" are left over and drawn last (1 Chron. 25:22-31).

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses supply the same picture from the other end. Adam Clarke, following the Targum, has "Moses the great scribe" write the names, and explains that because Eleazar's family was the more illustrious — Eleazar being Aaron's firstborn — one of its houses was drawn first, then one of Ithamar's, and so alternately. John Wesley traces the sequence step by step: the first lot fell to Eleazar, the second to Ithamar, the third to Eleazar, the fourth to Ithamar, and so on until Ithamar's eight families were spent, after which the remaining lots came forth for Eleazar's houses, "double in number" to Ithamar's. The witnesses do not really divide; they agree that a public scribe, before king and priests, recorded an alternating draw that gave the smaller house its full eight courses interleaved among the sixteen of the greater, and that the very keeping of the written record was part of the honesty of the thing.
7:
Now the list itself begins — "the first lot came forth to Jehoiarib" — and the witnesses treat the opening name and the drawing of the lots with a becoming plainness. The reformed John Gill says only that the first lot fell to Jehoiarib and the other twenty-three to the persons following, in their order. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, run the whole roll through without pause — Jehoiarib and Jedaiah, Harim and Seorim, Malchijah and Mijamin, and on to Delaiah and Maaziah as the four-and-twentieth — and then look ahead to the summary of v.19, where these are called their orderings "to come into the house of the LORD" under the rule ordained through Aaron.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, gives the first two names their pedigree. Jehoiarib and Jedaiah he links to the register of returning priests at 1 Chron. 9:10, and he remarks that from the house of Jehoiarib, first of all the courses, there would spring in a far later age the Maccabean princes who led Israel's revolt — so that the head of the list is also the fountain of a dynasty. He is careful, too, about the very verb: the lot "came forth," that is, came out from the urn in which the lots were shaken, as at Josh. 16:1 and Josh. 19:1. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says the same in a word: the lot "came out of the vessel in which all the lots were put together, and out of which they were severally taken."

The witnesses raise no dispute over so mechanical a verse, but they let its significance stand. The order of the whole priesthood, for a thousand years to come, was fixed in this moment by the tumbling of lots from a jar — Jehoiarib first, not by rank or merit but by the fall of the lot, and yet, by that very impartiality, first by the hand of God. That the same Jehoiarib should one day father the Hasmonean deliverers only deepens the point the witnesses have pressed from the chapter's head: what looks like a bare administrative roster is in fact the quiet outworking of providence, ordering the house of God and, hidden within it, the history of the nation.
8:
With the third and fourth lots the roll begins in earnest, and the only witness to linger over these two names is the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, who turns the dry register into a small gallery of portraits. Harim, the head of the third course, he derives from a root meaning "flat-nosed" or slit-nosed, and compares the Latin surname Naso worn by more than one Roman house — a family remembered, as it were, by the shape of a face. The name was no relic of a vanished world: it surfaces again among the priests who came up from the captivity, both in the great register of the returning exiles at Ezra 2:39 and among those who repaired the wall and sealed the covenant under Nehemiah at Neh. 3:11.

The fourth course fell to Seorim, whose name Ellicott reads as "barley," and so, by an easy extension, "bearded" — the bearded ears of the grain lending the man his by-name, much as the Latin Barbatus marked a bearded stock among the Romans. This name, unlike Harim's, is met nowhere else in Scripture; it flares once in the record and is gone. Ellicott's patient philology is more than antiquarian curiosity. It reminds the reader that behind every ordinal in the list — third, fourth, and the rest — stood a real household with a real name, often homely and even comical, and that the God who ordered the temple's service by lot ordered it not among abstractions but among men called "flat-nose" and "barley," the ordinary stuff of a nation's memory. That two such unglamorous names should be fixed for ever in the roll of those who ministered before the LORD is itself a quiet dignity. And that Harim's should still be borne by priests coming home from Babylon, centuries after David's urn was shaken, is a token written into the very register — the continuity Ellicott is at pains to trace from name to name — of how tenaciously the courses David founded clung to life across the long ruin of the exile, so that the third course was not merely instituted once but survived to serve again in a restored and humbler temple.
9:
The fifth and sixth courses bring two names that the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads as small windows into the piety and the play of Hebrew naming. Malchijah, on whom the fifth lot fell, he passes over quickly, noting only that the name recurs among the wall-builders of Nehemiah at Neh. 3:11 — a name compounded, as its form shows, of "king" and the divine name, one of the many priestly house-names that fold a confession of God's kingship into a single word.

It is over Mijamin, the sixth course, that Ellicott's philology grows most inventive. On its face the name "looks like 'on the right hand,'" the favoured and fortunate side; but he suspects a disguise. Perhaps, he suggests, the first syllable is a worn-down form of the Hebrew word for water, which was itself used metaphorically for "son" or offspring, as a stream issues from its source; and if so, the name would be equivalent to Benjamin, "son of the right hand" — the very name Jacob gave his last-born, and one that reappears among the priestly courses of the return at Neh. 12:5. The reader is left to weigh a conjecture, but the drift is clear enough: a name that seems merely to point rightward may carry within it the memory of a beloved son.

Ellicott is the lone voice here, and his interest is frankly grammatical rather than devotional; yet his notes are not idle. They keep before us that the twenty-four courses were not numbers but households, each bearing a name that meant something — here a king's name and there, perhaps, a son's — and that these same names endured to be counted again among the priests who rebuilt Jerusalem. The fifth and sixth lots, drawn in a single afternoon before David and the princes, fastened Malchijah and Mijamin into an order that would outlast the monarchy itself; and the patient tracing of their forms and their later reappearances is the grammatical-historical witness's way of showing that not a name in this register was arbitrary, and not one was lost. Ellicott offers the Benjamin derivation only as a guess — "perhaps," he says — and that very tentativeness is part of the witness's honesty, preferring a marked conjecture to a confident error, and leaving the reader free to weigh the water-and-son metaphor for himself.
10:
Of all the four-and-twenty names, none reaches so far beyond its own age as the pair drawn here, and both the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry pause upon the eighth course. The seventh lot fell to Hakkoz, a name Ellicott renders "the thorn," comparing the word for thorn at 1 Chron. 4:8 — a homely, prickly by-name of the sort the roll is full of. But it is the eighth course that matters. To Abijah, whom the Gospel spells "Abia," fell the eighth lot; and, as Ellicott plainly states, "to this class or course of the priests belonged Zacharias, the father of John the Baptist," pointing to the opening of the third Gospel at Luke 1:5.

Matthew Henry had already seized on the same connexion in his survey of the chapter, and drew from it the largest of conclusions. Among the twenty-four courses, he observes, the eighth is that of Abijah, which the Gospel names as the very course that Zacharias was of; and by this, he says, "it appears that these courses which David now settled, though interrupted perhaps in the bad reigns and long broken off by the captivity, yet continued in succession till the destruction of the second temple by the Romans." A single verse of Luke thus reaches back and touches this ancient roster: the old priest burning incense in his week of service, when the angel stood by the altar to promise him a son, was serving in the eighth course because a lot had assigned Abijah the eighth place a thousand years before.

Ellicott adds that Abijah's is a common enough priestly name, met again among the returning courses; but the witnesses do not divide, and the weight of the verse is undivided too. Henry makes it a marvel of providence and a comfort to faith: the God who ordered the courses by lot in David's day preserved them, through apostasy and exile and restoration, until they framed the very hour in which the forerunner of the Messiah was announced. The dry eighth line of a genealogical list becomes, in the witnesses' hands, a bridge from the temple David planned to the dawn of the Gospel — the course of Abia standing at the threshold of the New Testament, the last of the old order of priests keeping his turn at the altar while the new order, in John and in the Christ he heralded, was already at the door.
11:
The ninth and tenth lots yield a name that would one day be the sweetest in the world, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is careful to spell it out. The ninth course fell to Jeshuah, in Hebrew Yeshua, which the Greek renders Iesous — that is, Jesus — the very name, he notes, that meets us in the register of the return at Ezra 2:2. This form of the name, he adds, occurs only in Chronicles, Ezra, and Nehemiah, a mark of its late, post-exilic currency; and he records that the Syriac and Arabic versions read "Elisha" here instead, a variant he does not follow but faithfully sets down.

The tenth course fell to Shecaniah, a name Ellicott connects with the Davidic line at 1 Chron. 3:21 and glosses "Iah is a neighbour" — the LORD dwelling near his people — noting how common a name it became in the age after the exile. The two names set side by side are eloquent to the reader, though Ellicott himself keeps to the grammar: a priestly house called "the LORD is salvation" and another called "the LORD is near." Between them they say almost the whole hope of Israel.

No other witness speaks to these courses, and Ellicott's concern is strictly philological; yet his bare notes carry more than he presses. The name of the ninth course of Aaron's priests was, letter for letter, the name later given by an angel to the son of Mary, "for he shall save his people from their sins"; and the register that here spells Yeshua as a course of temple servants was pointing, all unawares, to the great high priest who would fulfil the whole system these courses served. The grammatical-historical witness asks only what the word meant and where else it stands; but in answering he lays bare how the ancient priesthood, in its very roll of names, kept unknowingly rehearsing the salvation and the nearness of God that the Gospel would at last make flesh. And Ellicott's care to set down the Syriac and Arabic reading "Elisha," while following neither, is characteristic of his method: he neither adopts a variant nor conceals it, but lays the manuscript evidence before the reader and lets the best-attested form of the ninth course's name stand.
12:
Two confessions of faith stand disguised as the eleventh and twelfth courses, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the sole witness here, unfolds them. The eleventh lot fell to Eliashib, a name he renders "God will restore," and connects with the Davidic register at 1 Chron. 3:24 — a word of hope built into a household's very title, spoken, perhaps, over a child born after loss. The twelfth course fell to Jakim, which Ellicott reads "He, that is, God or Jah, will establish," and reckons equivalent to the fuller names Eliakim and Jecamiah, comparing the latter at 1 Chron. 8:19.

Ellicott's interest is in the forms and their kindred, but the pairing he uncovers is worth dwelling on. Both names are little sentences about God, and both turn on his upholding power: the one declares that God "will restore," the other that God "will establish." The priestly houses of Israel, it appears, were often named not for their fathers' exploits but for their fathers' faith, each name a fragment of prayer or creed carried down the generations and now fixed, by the fall of a lot, into the eleventh and twelfth places of the temple's service.

There is no dispute to record, for only the one voice speaks; but the grammatical-historical method, pressed on so slight a verse, yields its own quiet fruit. To know that Jakim is but a shortened Eliakim, and that both mean "God establishes," is to see that even the driest link in the chain of courses testifies to the character of the God whom the courses served. The eleventh and twelfth priestly families bore about with them, in the names they answered to, the twin assurances that the God of Israel restores what is broken and makes firm what he has begun — assurances the returning exiles of Chronicles' own day would have read with peculiar comfort, seeing their restored and re-established temple staffed once more by houses whose very names had promised as much. That the twelfth course's name, Jakim, is but a clipped form of the fuller Eliakim and Jecamiah — the same confession lengthened or shortened without change of sense — shows besides how supple these theophoric names were in the mouths of the people, and how surely, under every variation, they kept saying the one thing: that it is God who upholds.
13:
The thirteenth and fourteenth names carry the reader from the sanctuary's canopy to a curious shadow of an older, darker word, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is the only guide. The thirteenth course fell to Huppah, a name meaning "covering" or "canopy," the very word used of the bridal or festal canopy at Isa. 4:5, where the LORD promises a defence over Zion; it occurs here only as a proper name, though Ellicott compares the kindred "Huppim" at 1 Chron. 7:12.

The fourteenth course, spelled Joshebeab, is stranger. As it stands, Ellicott parses it "May the Father — that is, God — cause to lead captive," a fierce little petition for victory over enemies. But the Greek text, he notes, read the name quite differently, as Eshbaal, "man of Baal," and the Latin followed with "Isbaab." Ellicott records the variant without embracing it, but it opens a small window on how such theophoric names could shift, and how a form that once may have contained the name of Baal — as Saul's own son was called Eshbaal before piety softened it to Ish-bosheth — could be reshaped in transmission into a name honouring the true God as "Father."

Only Ellicott speaks, and his aim is textual rather than edifying; yet the two names set together are instructive. Huppah's "canopy" recalls the sheltering presence of God over his sanctuary, while Joshebeab's disputed form shows the very letters of a priestly house wavering between a memory of Baal and a confession of the divine Fatherhood. The grammatical-historical witness, by weighing the Hebrew against the Greek and the Latin, does not merely satisfy curiosity; he lets us see the roll of the courses as a living text, its names worn and copied and sometimes half-misread across the centuries, and yet preserved — the thirteenth and fourteenth houses still recognisable, still numbered among those appointed to serve beneath the canopy of the LORD's protection. That the Greek and the Hebrew should differ over a single priestly name is a small specimen of the labour the grammatical-historical school makes its own — the patient collation of version against version by which the earliest and truest form of an ancient text is slowly recovered from the drift of the copyists.
14:
The fifteenth and sixteenth courses wear names of gladness, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the sole witness who speaks to the verse, draws out their brightness. The fifteenth lot fell to Bilgah, a name he renders "smiling," or beaming with cheerfulness, and compares to Isaac "the laugher," whose name too was born of laughter; the form recurs, he notes, as "Bilgai" among the sealers of the covenant at Neh. 10:9 and again among the priestly courses of the return at Neh. 12:5. The sixteenth course fell to Immer, a name Ellicott takes to mean "perhaps a lamb," and connects with the priestly house named at 1 Chron. 9:10 and with the father of the Pashhur who would one day smite Jeremiah at Jer. 20:1.

Ellicott's method is philological, but the names he uncovers repay the attention. A course called "smiling" and a course called "lamb" stand among the servants of the altar — the one breathing the cheerfulness that Isaac's name had carried, the other, if the derivation holds, quietly apt for a priestly family, since it was lambs above all that such men offered morning and evening before the LORD. The reappearance of Bilgah among the returning priests is one more thread in the continuity Ellicott traces through the whole roll: the fifteenth course, instituted by David's lot, was still counted and still named when the exiles came home.

No second voice contends, and the source at this point carries a stray fragment from another commentator that speaks of a prophecy against Jeroboam's altar — matter belonging wholly to a different book, and rightly passed over. What the genuine witness leaves us is small but clean: two more households, "smiling" Bilgah and "lamb" Immer, fixed by lot into the fifteenth and sixteenth places, their homely and hopeful names preserved in the register and echoing on, past the fall of the first temple, into the priesthood of the second. Yet the record carries its own quiet warning: the house of Immer would, long after, give Israel the Pashhur who smote the prophet Jeremiah and shut him in the stocks — a darker note struck beneath the cheerful etymology, and a reminder that a name of good omen was no guarantee of a good heart in those who bore it.
15:
The seventeenth and eighteenth lots fall on two of the strangest names in the roll, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, alone here, does not soften them. The seventeenth course fell to Hezir, a name he bluntly translates "hog" — an unlikely title for a priest, yet a genuine one, borne again among the sealers of Nehemiah's covenant at Neh. 10:21. He records that the Syriac and Arabic versions, perhaps recoiling from so unclean a word, read "Ahaziah" instead; but the Vulgate and the Septuagint alike attest "Hezir," and Ellicott lets the harder reading stand.

The eighteenth course fell to Aphses, which Ellicott gives in its Hebrew form ha-pizzez, "the scatterer" or "the disperser," a name found here and nowhere else in Scripture. He notes the tangle of versional spellings — the Septuagint's Aphesse, the Vulgate's Aphses, the Syriac and Arabic Phasin — the kind of scatter, one might say, that the name itself describes, as if the very word had broken into fragments in the hands of the copyists.

There is no debate to record, for the single witness only reports what the name means and how the versions render it; yet even here the grammatical-historical labour is not wasted. That the seventeenth priestly house should be called "hog" is a salutary reminder that the roll of the courses was no idealised list of pious epithets but a real record of real families, some of them bearing names as rough and unflattering as any village nickname. Ellicott's refusal to emend "Hezir" away, against the softening of the Syriac and Arabic, is of a piece with his whole handling of the chapter: he will follow the best-attested text wherever it leads, trusting that the God who chose these houses to serve him was not ashamed to number among them a family called "hog" and another called "the scatterer," and to keep both names in his book long after the men who bore them were dust. The recoil of the Syriac and Arabic from so coarse a word as "hog" into the dignified "Ahaziah" is itself instructive: it betrays the impulse of later scribes to tidy Scripture, and Ellicott's countervailing rule — to follow the harder, better-attested reading — is precisely what guards the text from being smoothed into something its author never wrote.
16:
With the nineteenth and twentieth courses the register sounds again its favourite note — the name that is really a prayer — and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the one witness here, tunes the ear to it. The nineteenth lot fell to Pethahiah, a name he renders "Iah openeth," that is, sets free, and compares to Jephthah, "He, Iah, openeth"; the form recurs, he adds, among the men of Ezra's day at Ezra 10:23. It is a name of release, fit for a people who knew what it was to be shut up and then opened out to liberty.

The twentieth course fell to Jehezekel, which Ellicott identifies at once as the Hebrew Yehezqel — the name we know, through its Greek dress, as Ezekiel, "God strengthens." That a priestly course should bear the name of the great priest-prophet of the exile is not, in the order of time, a borrowing from him; but the coincidence lets the reader feel how deeply the priesthood and the prophetic hope were woven of the same threads, the twentieth house of Aaron's courses named, like the watchman of the exile, for the strengthening power of God.

No dissenting voice is raised, and Ellicott keeps to his etymologies; yet the pair he sets before us continues the quiet sermon the names themselves preach. "Iah openeth" and "God strengthens" — the nineteenth and twentieth courses confess between them the God who both liberates and upholds. The grammatical-historical witness, by tracing each name to its root and its recurrences, does more than gratify the antiquarian: he shows that the roll of the courses is, among other things, a litany of the divine attributes, each household answering to a title that celebrates some mercy of the LORD, so that the mere reading of the list, name after name, becomes an unintended act of praise. The name Pethahiah would have rung with a special sweetness after the captivity, when the God who "openeth" had unbarred the prison of Babylon and set his people free to come home; and that a priestly course should stand under the name later made glorious by the watchman Ezekiel binds the ministry of the altar to the ministry of the word, both alike confessing that it is God who strengthens.
17:
The twenty-first and twenty-second lots set a famous temple word among the courses, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, sole witness, points it out. The twenty-first course fell to Jachin, a name meaning "He, that is, Jah, setteth up" or "maketh firm," which Ellicott notes is the same name as Jehoiachin and traces back to the son of Simeon at Gen. 46:10. The reader can hardly miss the further resonance, though Ellicott names the reference plainly: Jachin was also the name Solomon gave to the right-hand pillar of bronze at the porch of the temple, "he shall establish," as recorded at 1 Kings 7:21 — so that a priestly course and one of the great pillars of the house bore one and the same confession of God's establishing power.

The twenty-second course fell to Gamul, a name Ellicott gives as "weaned," met here only as a proper name, and compares to the weaned child that plays by the serpent's den in the peaceable kingdom of Isa. 11:8. It is a gentler, more domestic word than most in the list, drawn from the nursery rather than the battlefield or the confession of faith.

There is no controversy to weigh, for only Ellicott speaks; but his two notes, set together, keep the pattern the whole roll has been tracing. Jachin, "God establishes," binds the twenty-first course to the very pillar of the sanctuary and to the settled hope that God will make his house and his people firm; Gamul, "weaned," touches the tenderness of the same God who nurses and then weans his children. The grammatical-historical witness, in fixing the meaning and the kindred of each name, lets even these late courses of the list testify — the one to God's strength to establish, the other to his fatherly care — so that the twenty-first and twenty-second households take their appointed places not as bare numbers but as small standing witnesses to the character of the God they were set apart to serve. The pairing of a serving course with a standing pillar is more than a coincidence of names: both proclaimed, at the very door of the house, that its permanence rested not on bronze nor on priestly succession but on the God who "setteth up"; and that a course should also be called "weaned" sets beside that public strength the tenderest of images, as if the roll would confess God's might and his fatherly care in a single breath.
18:
The last two lots close the roll of the priests, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the only witness, gives the twenty-third and twenty-fourth courses their due. To Delaiah fell the twenty-third lot, a name Ellicott renders "Jah draws out," that is, sets free — a common name in the age after the exile, which he links with the Davidic register at 1 Chron. 3:24 and with the prince who feared for Jeremiah's scroll at Jer. 36:12. Like Pethahiah before it, the name confesses the God who delivers his people, drawing them out of the pit and the prison-house.

The twenty-fourth and final course fell to Maaziah, a name met here only. Ellicott suspects it should perhaps be read "Maadiah," the form found among the returning priestly courses at Neh. 12:5, and records the scatter of versional spellings — the Syriac's Ma'adyah, the Arabic's Mi'diyya, the Septuagint's Maasai, the Vulgate's Maaziau — the uncertainty that so often attends the last, least-copied name in a list.

No other voice is raised, and Ellicott's task is textual to the end; yet the closing of the roll is a fit moment to see what his patient labour has shown across these bare verses. Four-and-twenty households, drawn out one by one from David's urn, stand named in order from Jehoiarib to Maaziah, and nearly every name, under the grammatical-historical eye, proves to be either a little portrait or a little prayer. The twenty-third course confesses that "Jah draws out," and even the doubtful twenty-fourth, whatever its true spelling, takes its place to complete the sacred number. With Maaziah the list is full: the whole priesthood of Israel is set in its courses, ready, as the next verse will say, "to come into the house of the LORD" according to the rule ordained through Aaron. Ellicott's willingness to leave the last name in doubt — "perhaps Maadiah," he says, setting down four rival spellings without choosing — is the mark of his whole method: he would sooner report an honest uncertainty than impose a false tidiness. Yet the doubt touches only the spelling, never the substance; the twenty-fourth course stood, whatever its exact name, and the sacred number was complete.
19:
The roll of priests ends with a summary, and the witnesses read it as a statement of both order and authority. The reformed John Gill takes it plainly: "these were the orderings of them," their classes or courses into which they were divided. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the verse more exactly, "These were their classes for their service," and unfolds the rule it invokes. "According to their manner, under Aaron," he corrects to "according to their rule, ordained through Aaron," pointing back to the ordinances given at Num. 2:1 and Num. 4:1; the sacerdotal functions were all fixed, and each of the twenty-four classes undertook the weekly discharge of them in rotation, beginning on the sabbath, as the changing of the temple guard at 2 Kings 11:9 and 2 Chron. 23:8 presupposes. And Ellicott adds a striking testimony to the arrangement's endurance: Josephus, writing in the first century, "declares that the arrangements of David lasted down to his own day."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fills in the rhythm of the service. In this order and method the priests were to perform the offices of the temple, "to come into the temple every sabbath-day, and to continue there till the next sabbath, when they were relieved by others" — a week's turn of duty for each course, week after week through the year. And Wesley explains the last clause, "under Aaron their father": the courses served under the direction of the high priests, whom the text calls Aaron because each represented Aaron's person and executed his office, and "their father" because of the authority which by God's appointment the high priest held over them. Adam Clarke says the same in brief: "they followed the order and plans laid down by Aaron during his lifetime."

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse gathers up the whole section that precedes it. What the lot had scattered into four-and-twenty courses is here bound again into one settled system of service, resting on ancient authority — the rule ordained through Aaron — and destined, on Josephus' word, to outlast the monarchy, the exile, and the centuries, until the temple itself should fall. The dry rubric "these were the orderings of them" thus closes the priestly roll on a note of permanence: an order divinely appointed, faithfully kept, and still standing when the Gospel opened.
20:
The chapter turns from the priests to the rest of the Levites, and the witnesses mark both the shift and its purpose. The reformed John Gill notes that these were the sons of Levi "who were not priests, only Levites," and rehearses the names that follow — of the Kohathite line, Shubael and his son Jehdeiah, Rehabiah and his son Isshiah, the Izharites, the sons of Hebron and of Uzziel — observing that most of them stood already in the muster of 1 Chron. 23. Keil and Delitzsch, of the grammatical-historical school, read the opening word "the rest" or "those left over" as pointing to the Levites who remained after the priests had been dealt with, and they are careful to limit its scope: the catalogue that follows treats only of the Levites employed about the worship, the singers, the doorkeepers, and the keepers of the treasures being separately enumerated in the chapters at 1 Chron. 25 and 1 Chron. 26.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, notes that the list "begins with the Kohathite heads, omitting the Gershonites" of 1 Chron. 23:7 — perhaps, he suggests, through a gap in the Chronicler's manuscript authority. He identifies Shubael as a variant of Shebuel, the grandson of Moses named at 1 Chron. 23:16, the name here standing for a whole Levitical house whose head in David's day was Jehdeiah, "Jah gladdens" — a name met again at 1 Chron. 26:30.

It is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who draws the doctrine. These Levites, he observes, were to attend the priests, and are here set out as heads of twenty-four courses of their own, answering to the twenty-four courses of the priests; and the text says they cast lots "over against their brethren" — not their lords — the sons of Aaron, who were not to lord it over God's clergy, in the very spirit the apostle forbids at 1 Pet. 5:3. That the whole disposal might be of the Lord, the principal fathers cast lots over against their younger brethren, so that the elder house came level with the younger, taking their place not by seniority but as God directed by the lot. And in this Henry hears the Gospel: "in Christ no difference is made between bond and free, elder and younger. The younger brethren, if faithful and sincere, shall be no less acceptable to Christ than the principal fathers." The witnesses converge on a single lesson — that the servants of the sanctuary, priest and Levite, elder and younger, were bound together not by rank but by a common calling and a common Lord.
21:
The catalogue of Levitical houses begins with the line of Amram, and the two grammatical-historical witnesses read the verse as much for what it proves as for what it names. Keil and Delitzsch identify Rehabiah's father's-house as the one connected with Eliezer, the second son of Moses named at 1 Chron. 23:16, and note that of the sons of this Rehabiah the head was Isshiah. Charles Ellicott renders the phrase carefully — "for the sons of Rehabiah, the chief was Isshiah" — and recalls that the parallel muster at 1 Chron. 23:17 says only that "the sons of Rehabiah were very many," so that the present verse advances the record by naming the particular head David set over them.

Keil and Delitzsch press a subtler point that governs this whole second half of the chapter. The heads named here in vv.20-22 — Jehdeiah, Isshiah, Jahath — are not to be found in the earlier list of 1 Chron. 23; and this, they argue, is "a clear proof" that the earlier chapter treats of the fathers'-houses, while the present section treats of the official classes of the Levites, the working divisions David formed for the temple service. The same names could not simply be copied over, because the two lists are doing two different things.

The witnesses do not divide; the two grammatical-historical voices reinforce one another. What might look like a bare repetition of Levitical names is in fact a distinct register — not the family tree of chapter twenty-three but the roster of service-classes, each headed by the man who led it in David's day. Rehabiah, sprung from Moses through Eliezer, and now represented by his chief Isshiah, takes his place in that roster; and the very novelty of the head's name, absent from the earlier chapter, is the quiet evidence Keil and Delitzsch seize upon to show that the Chronicler knew exactly what he was about, giving first the houses and now the classes, that both the descent and the duty of the Levites might be on record. The point is not pedantic. Were the two lists mere duplicates, the Chronicler might be charged with careless repetition; but the surfacing of new heads absent from the earlier chapter shows him doing, deliberately, two distinct things — giving first the pedigree of the Levites and then their working divisions for the temple.
22:
The list moves to the Izharite branch, and the two grammatical-historical witnesses use the verse to press a point about the whole genealogy's grammar. Keil and Delitzsch identify the father's-house as Shelomoth, named at 1 Chron. 23:18, whose sons stood under the head Jahath; and once more they observe that this head, Jahath, is not met in the earlier chapter — fresh confirmation that the present section registers the official classes, not the fathers'-houses already listed.

Charles Ellicott draws attention to the form of the name. "For the Izharites," he renders, and remarks that the gentilic form of the designation — "the Izharites" rather than simply "Izhar" — is a signal worth heeding: it "indicates that Shubael, Rehabiah, and others of these proper names are likewise names of houses or clans," not merely of single men. The genealogy, that is, is shading continually from persons into peoples; a name that reads like an individual's often stands for a whole kindred descended from him. Ellicott notes too that while Shelomoth was named in the earlier chapter, its chief Jahath was not.

The witnesses agree, and their agreement is itself the substance of the verse. What the casual reader might take for a simple pedigree — this man begat that man — the grammatical-historical eye reads as a register of clans and their appointed heads. The Izharites, sprung from Izhar the brother of Amram, are gathered under Shelomoth as their house and under Jahath as their serving head; and the plural, gentilic form of the name is the small grammatical clue by which Ellicott unlocks the character of the whole list. It is not, at bottom, a chronicle of individuals but a muster of the Levitical families organised for the work of the sanctuary, each clan set in its class and each class given its chief, so that the service of the house of God might go forward in good order. This single grammatical observation reshapes the reading of the whole second half of the chapter: where the eye sees a chain of fathers and sons, Ellicott bids it see a map of kindreds, the "sons" of a man standing for the clans that traced themselves to him, and the "head" named beside them for the living chief who led that clan in David's muster.
23:
At the sons of Hebron the text grows ragged, and both grammatical-historical witnesses acknowledge that the verse comes down to us damaged. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the verse treats of the Hebronites, as is clear from the parallel at 1 Chron. 23:19, "but here the text is imperfect." Instead of naming the chiefs of the classes into which David divided the four Hebronite fathers'-houses, the verse gives only the four familiar names — Jeriah, Amariah, Jahaziel, Jekameam — repeated exactly as in the earlier chapter, and even introduced, awkwardly, by "and the sons of," with no antecedent supplied. Bertheau, they note, would mend the gap by inserting the name "Hebron" after "and the sons of," a correction they judge "probably correct," though not quite beyond doubt.

Charles Ellicott states the case as bluntly: "the Hebrew text is here mutilated," and "our translators have emended it from 1 Chron. 23:19." The names of the houses or classes are given, he says, but not those of their heads — the very information the section elsewhere supplies has here dropped out.

The witnesses do not dispute the fact of the corruption; where they show a becoming restraint is over its cause. Keil and Delitzsch confess it "impossible to ascertain" whether the heads' names were lost by a later copyist or were simply never contained in the source the Chronicler used. Here the grammatical-historical method reaches its honest limit and says so. Rather than invent the missing heads, the witnesses record the defect, note how the Authorized translators quietly repaired the sense from the parallel passage, and leave the question of the loss open. The verse thus becomes a small lesson in the humility of the interpreter: the sons of Hebron kept their place among the serving Levites, but the particular chiefs of their classes have slipped through a tear in the record, and the faithful commentator marks the tear rather than papering over it. It is worth marking how differently this reticence reads from the confident harmonising of a lesser hand. Faced with a hole in the record, Keil and Delitzsch neither fill it from imagination nor deny that it is there; they weigh Bertheau's conjecture, grant it probable, and stop — leaving the four Hebronite houses their names, and their vanished heads their silence.
24:
The Kohathite roll closes with the house of Uzziel, and the two grammatical-historical witnesses gather up the count. Keil and Delitzsch trace the descent: of the sons of Uzziel, Michah; of the sons of Michah, Shamir; while Michah's brother was Isshiah, of whose sons Zechariah was head. Charles Ellicott sets the verse beside the parallel at 1 Chron. 23:20, and clears up a spelling: the "Jesiah" of that chapter is the same Hebrew name as the "Isshiah" here, and should be pointed alike in both places. Shamir and Zechariah, he notes, are the heads of the sons of Michah and of the sons of Isshiah respectively.

Ellicott then draws the tally that gives the verse its interest. Only five heads of the nine Kohathite houses, he counts, are actually mentioned in this section — Jehdeiah, Isshiah, Jahath, Shamir, and Zechariah — the rest going unnamed. Keil and Delitzsch reckon on the same problem from a wider angle: the heads named through the whole Levitical section come to fifteen in all, and if each undivided father's-house formed but a single class, there would be only fifteen classes rather than the expected twenty-four; yet, they add, it is "quite conceivable that many of the fathers'-houses were so numerous as to form more than one class," which would make up the number.

The witnesses do not divide; both are wrestling honestly with a list that gives fewer heads than the scheme of twenty-four seems to require. Their shared conclusion is not to force the text but to allow that a single large house might have furnished several serving-classes, so that fifteen named heads could still stand behind a full four-and-twenty courses. The house of Uzziel, with Michah and Isshiah and their heads Shamir and Zechariah, thus completes the Kohathite contingent; and the very gap between the heads named and the courses expected becomes, in the grammatical-historical handling, not an error to be hidden but a feature of an ancient administrative record whose logic the commentator patiently reconstructs. The reasoning is a fine specimen of the grammatical-historical temper: rather than emend the figures to force an agreement, the witnesses let the recorded heads stand and supply the missing classes by a reasonable inference — fifteen chiefs, some of them leading houses large enough to be split in two, could well have furnished the full four-and-twenty courses that the parallel institutions require.
26:
The register turns to the Merarite line, and the witnesses meet at once a textual snag and a notable silence. Adam Clarke, of the wesleyan-arminian school, remarks on the silence first: "it is remarkable that not a word is here spoken of the family of Gershom" — the whole Gershonite branch of Levi passed over, whether by design or by a defect in the record. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, opens the section (running through v.30) as the roll of the Merarite heads, to be compared with the earlier muster at 1 Chron. 23:21.

The snag is the word "Beno." Ellicott points out that "Beno" is simply the Hebrew for "his son," and "can hardly be a proper name." The clause, he argues, should be joined to what follows in v.27 and read: "The sons of Jaaziah his son — that is, sons of Merari belonging to Jaaziah his son — were Shoham, and Zaccur, and Ibri." On this reading Jaaziah appears as a third son of Merari, otherwise unmentioned, and the verse implies that in David's day there existed a group of Merarite houses calling themselves "sons of Jaaziah." Keil and Delitzsch, by contrast, transcribe the verse with "Beno" standing in the list as though a name among the sons, so that a small disagreement opens: is "Beno" a genuine name, or the misread Hebrew for "his son"?

The witnesses do not resolve it to a certainty, and Ellicott himself grants that his construction requires dropping the conjunction before "Shoham" in the next verse. But the drift of the grammatical-historical reading is clear, and Clarke's observation frames it: the Merarite portion of the list is both incomplete, wanting the Gershonites altogether, and textually bruised at its very head, where a common word for "his son" has perhaps been mistaken for the name of a man. The faithful course, the witnesses imply, is to read "sons of Jaaziah his son" and to recognise in these Merarite families — Shoham, Zaccur, Ibri — a real branch of the tribe, gathered under Jaaziah, taking their place among the serving Levites even where the record has come down to us frayed.
27:
The verse continues the Merarite roll, and the two grammatical-historical witnesses both defend its genuineness against those who would strike it out. Keil and Delitzsch read on through the branch — "the sons of Merari by Jaaziah: Beno, and Shoham, and Zaccur, and Ibri" — carrying the line down toward the sons of Mahli and Mushi that follow. Charles Ellicott lingers on two of the names: Shoham, which means "onyx," and Ibri, which means "Hebrew," neither of which "occurs as an individual name elsewhere"; yet, he insists, "there is no reason to doubt their genuineness." He notes plainly that "some commentators pronounce vv.26-27 spurious," a later insertion into the text — but adds that they do so "against the evidence of the ancient versions," which attest the passage.

Keil and Delitzsch, at the close of the whole section, draw out the theology latent in the drily-worded subscription. The lot that ordered these Levitical houses, they note with the Vulgate, divided all alike — tam majores quam minores; omnes sors aequaliter dividebat, "both the greater and the lesser: the lot divided all equally." The head of a father's-house drew his place by the same impartial lot as his younger brethren, so that dignity of birth conferred no advantage in the service of God.

The witnesses do not divide; they stand together to keep a threatened passage in the text and to read its meaning aright. Against the critics who would excise these Merarite verses as a late intrusion, Ellicott sets the united testimony of the versions and the intrinsic plausibility of names like "onyx" and "Hebrew"; and Keil and Delitzsch, refusing to let the passage be dismissed as mere lumber, find in it the same equalising grace that has run through the chapter from the priests onward. The obscure Merarite families — Shoham, Zaccur, Ibri, gathered under Jaaziah — are neither spurious nor negligible: they are real houses of Levi, set by the same even-handed lot into the service of the sanctuary, the greater and the lesser sharing alike. Nor is the dispute idle: to strike out these verses would be to thin the Merarite roll and to lose the very names — "onyx" and "Hebrew" — that give it its concreteness, and Ellicott's appeal to the united versions is the proper answer to a criticism resting on nothing firmer than the strangeness of a name.
28:
A small Merarite house is recorded as having died out, and the witnesses read the notice as a quiet instance of how the tribe's families were consolidated. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, renders the Hebrew literally, "To Mahli, Eleazar; and there became not to him sons," comparing the same fact as stated at 1 Chron. 23:22. The clan of Eleazar, he explains, "did not branch out into new clans, but, being few in number, amalgamated with that of Kish-Jerahmeel" — absorbed, that is, into the neighbouring house rather than continuing as a class of its own.

Keil and Delitzsch, treating the Merarite branch as a whole, note the same thing from the side of the surviving line: since Mahli's son Eleazar had no sons, only Jerahmeel, sprung from Mahli's second son Kish, is named as head of the class formed out of Mahli's descendants. The one branch failing, the other carries the house.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse, slight as it is, fits the pattern the whole catalogue has traced. Not every Levitical family flourished; some, like Eleazar's, came to an end for want of sons and had to be joined to a stronger kindred that the name and the service might not be wholly lost. There is no lament in the record and none in the witnesses' handling of it — only the sober arithmetic of a tribe keeping its ranks full: where a house failed of male heirs, it was merged with another, and the class it might have formed alone was carried instead under the head of its cousins. Even in so minor a notice the grammatical-historical eye finds the logic of the list, and lets the childless house of Eleazar take its place, absorbed but not forgotten, among the serving families of Merari. There is a gentle pathos in the notice that the witnesses neither dwell on nor erase: a whole priestly family, set apart to serve the living God, simply had no sons, and its distinct existence ended within a generation or two. Yet the name Eleazar was not struck from the book; it stands here still, joined to Kish-Jerahmeel, its service continued under a cousin's head.
29:
The line broken off in the previous verse is here carried on through the other son, and the witnesses read the notice for the way it keeps the Merarite house alive. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, renders it literally, "To Kish, the sons of Kish, Jerahmeel," observing that the plural "sons of Kish" is used just as at 1 Chron. 23:22, even though a single name, Jerahmeel, follows — the idiom of the register preferring the plural of the house to the singular of the man.

Keil and Delitzsch, reading the Merarite section as a whole, make Jerahmeel the head of the class that gathered up Mahli's descendants. Since Mahli's elder son Eleazar had failed of male heirs, it is through Kish, the second son, and his son Jerahmeel that the house of Mahli continues and takes its place among the serving Levites; Jerahmeel stands, in effect, for the whole surviving strength of that branch.

There is no dispute between the witnesses; the two grammatical-historical voices simply confirm one another. The verse completes the small drama of the two previous notices: Eleazar's line ends childless and is absorbed, while Kish's line, in Jerahmeel, carries the family forward. The plural "sons of Kish" for a single named heir is, as Ellicott sees, no carelessness but the settled manner of these lists, which speak habitually of houses rather than of individuals; and Jerahmeel, that lone heir, becomes the head under whom the united remnant of Mahli's descendants was reckoned. So the register, with its bare "to Kish, the sons of Kish, Jerahmeel," quietly secures the continuance of a Levitical house that had all but failed, and sets it, through its one surviving branch, in the ordered service of the sanctuary. The idiom repays a moment's thought: a register that will say "the sons of Kish" where only one son survived is a register more concerned with houses than with headcounts, content to name the family and let the single heir stand for the whole. Where the previous verses recorded a line's near-extinction, this one records its rescue — the branch that might have ended in Eleazar lives on in Jerahmeel, and the chain of Levitical service, so nearly broken, holds.
30:
The Levitical roll ends, and the witnesses read its closing verse as both a last set of names and a subscription over the whole. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, notes that the sons of Mushi — Mahli, Eder, and Jerimoth — are given exactly as at 1 Chron. 23:23, and, as with the Hebronites of v.23, "only the names of the houses or classes are mentioned, without those of the chiefs." Keil and Delitzsch carry the same three names in their survey of the Merarite branch, closing the catalogue of the Levitical houses.

It is over the final clause that Ellicott grows expansive. "These were the sons of the Levites after the house of their fathers," he renders "according to their father-houses," and finds in this subscription a proof of the section's original completeness: it shows that "the original of vv.20-30 contained a complete catalogue of the Levitical houses or clans, exclusive of the Aaronites." Whatever gaps and mutilations now scar the text — the missing Gershonites, the lost Hebronite heads, the bruised opening of the Merarite roll — the summary formula testifies that the writer meant to set down the Levitical families in full.

Ellicott adds the honest caveat that governs his whole reading of the chapter: "how far the apparent defects of the present Hebrew text reproduce those of its archetype, and how far they are due to errors of transcription, cannot now be decided." The witnesses do not divide; they close the list as they have handled it throughout — naming what the text names, marking where heads have dropped away, and refusing to pretend to a certainty the evidence will not bear. The sons of Mushi take their place, the subscription seals the roll, and the grammatical-historical labour ends where it must, with a complete design partially preserved: a full muster of the serving Levites once written down, now come to us worn at the edges, but still legible enough to show the order David gave to the house of God. The candour of that final caveat is the fitting close to a chapter so scarred by loss and repair: Ellicott will claim neither more nor less than the evidence allows — the design complete, the execution come down to us broken, and the exact measure of the damage now past knowing — yet what survives is enough to show the shape of the whole.
31:
The chapter closes as it opened, with the casting of lots, and the witnesses read the final verse as the seal of a single principle — that in the service of God rank yields to the impartial hand of heaven. The reformed John Gill explains that these Levites, "being divided into twenty-four classes also, cast lots" to determine which class should answer to and serve alongside the first course of the priests, which the second, and so on — and this "in the same solemn and public manner" as the priests had done. The Levitical courses were thus knit, course for course, to the priestly ones they were to assist.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke says the same: the Levites, in twenty-four orders, "were appointed by lot to serve under the twenty-four orders of the priests," the first order of Levites under the first order of priests, and so throughout. He cites Bishop Patrick, that "both elder and younger had their places by lot, not by seniority of houses" — those of greater dignity drawing lots against those of less, and taking their courses according to the lot they drew — and adds, with a candour worth noting, "we are very little interested in the subject."

Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, weighs the wording. The phrase "over against their brethren" he renders "just like, in the same way as their brethren, the priests," the same compound preposition that recurs at 1 Chron. 26:12 and carries the sense of "parallel with" at 2 Sam. 16:13. This expression, he judges, shows that the preceding list did not include all the Levites but only those who assisted the priests in the temple — the twenty-four thousand set apart for the work at 1 Chron. 23:4 — which may be why the Gershonite houses were omitted. "The principal fathers over against their younger brethren" he reads "the chief just like his younger brother," meaning that all the Levitical houses received their position by lot, senior and junior branches alike; and though the text does not expressly say the classes were twenty-four, he thinks it "highly probable," both from its own data and from the analogy of the priests and the singers of 1 Chron. 25. The witnesses converge on the chapter's abiding lesson: the Levites, like the priests before them, were set in their courses not by the privilege of birth but by the fall of the lot, so that the whole service of the house of God, from the sons of Aaron to the least of their attendant brethren, rested on an order that was, at every point, the LORD's own disposing.
